Attempts at Connecting Fluctuations in the Gut Microbiome with the Development of Human Hepatocellular Carcinoma

Juliette Majeske and Amelia Worth

2025-04-30

    • 11.0.1 Session Info

1 About the dataset

The authors used NGS RNA-seq methods to identify molecular signatures in Hepatocellular Carcinoma tissues that correlate with and could indicate survival. 32 sets of paired HCC and non-tumor liver tissue samples were retrospectively obtained from patients with HCC cancer. 32 pairs of samples were then processed for RNA-seq with Illumina Hiseq-150. No biological sample duplicates were used. Hisat2 2.1.0 and gfold v1.1.4 were used to analyze the resulting RNA-seq data for differences in gene expression (both up-regulations down-regulation) between tumor and nontumor tissues. 12 samples (6 tumor and 6 nontumor) were selected from the original dataset of 32 paired samples. Paired-end processing resulted in 24 FASTQ that were then recombined into 12 samples for analysis. All 12 samples were of good quality and did not require additional trimming.

Huang, H., Ren, Z., Gao, X., Hu, X., Zhou, Y., Jiang, J., Lu, H., Yin, S., Ji, J., Zhou, L., & Zheng, S. (2020). Integrated analysis of microbiome and host transcriptome reveals correlations between gut microbiota and clinical outcomes in HBV-related hepatocellular carcinoma. Genome medicine, 12(1), 102. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13073-020-00796-5

GEO accession number: GSE138485

Green Trail GOAL: We set out with the goal to replicate Figure 4B and 4C, which we weren’t able to do. As the final result, we generated boxplots that displayed the number of reads in our sample library that mapped to several chosen genes (CD6, MAPK10, GPBAR1, and ABCC4).

2 Set working directory

3 Load Libraries

4 DESeq2

4.1 Prep pieces for dds object

#load in the htseq count data as a table
sampleTable <- read.csv("CORRECT_csv_allcounts2.csv", header = T)

head(sampleTable)
##           samplename                             filename condition replicate
## 1      Tumor T_b_016 SRR10509512_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         1
## 2      Tumor T_c_022 SRR10509513_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         2
## 3      Tumor T_d_024 SRR10509514_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         3
## 4      Tumor T_f_029 SRR10509516_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         4
## 5 Non Tumor T_aa_015 SRR10509520_sorted.gene_id.count.txt  nontumor         1
## 6 Non Tumor T_bb_016 SRR10509521_sorted.gene_id.count.txt  nontumor         2
#convert to data frame 
sampleTable <- data.frame(sampleTable)
head(sampleTable)
##           samplename                             filename condition replicate
## 1      Tumor T_b_016 SRR10509512_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         1
## 2      Tumor T_c_022 SRR10509513_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         2
## 3      Tumor T_d_024 SRR10509514_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         3
## 4      Tumor T_f_029 SRR10509516_sorted.gene_id.count.txt     tumor         4
## 5 Non Tumor T_aa_015 SRR10509520_sorted.gene_id.count.txt  nontumor         1
## 6 Non Tumor T_bb_016 SRR10509521_sorted.gene_id.count.txt  nontumor         2
str(sampleTable)
## 'data.frame':    9 obs. of  4 variables:
##  $ samplename: chr  "Tumor T_b_016" "Tumor T_c_022" "Tumor T_d_024" "Tumor T_f_029" ...
##  $ filename  : chr  "SRR10509512_sorted.gene_id.count.txt" "SRR10509513_sorted.gene_id.count.txt" "SRR10509514_sorted.gene_id.count.txt" "SRR10509516_sorted.gene_id.count.txt" ...
##  $ condition : chr  "tumor" "tumor" "tumor" "tumor" ...
##  $ replicate : int  1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4 5

Next, we want to specify the condition column as a factor. By doing so, this will allow us to categorize the data and store it as a level.

#?DESeqDataSetFromHTSeqCount
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromHTSeqCount(sampleTable = sampleTable,
                                  directory = "sorted_counts/",
                                  design = ~ condition)

4.2 Pre-filtering

# keep only rows that have a number of reads greater than 10
keep <- rowSums(counts(dds)) >= 10
dds <- dds[keep,]

4.3 Run DESeq

# define out two study groups at "tumor" anmd "nontumor"
dds$condition <- relevel(dds$condition, ref = "nontumor")

5 Data Visualization, Part 1

5.1 Run a PCA plot

vstcounts <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)
#pdf(file = "PCA_plot_TNT_RNAseq.pdf", height = 6, width = 6)
pcaData <- plotPCA(vstcounts, intgroup=c("condition"), returnData=TRUE)
## using ntop=500 top features by variance
percentVar <- round(100 * attr(pcaData, "percentVar"))

ggplot(pcaData, aes(PC1, PC2, color=condition)) +
  geom_point(size=2) +
  xlab(paste0("PC1: ",percentVar[1],"% variance")) +
  ylab(paste0("PC2: ",percentVar[2],"% variance")) + 
  coord_fixed() 

#dev.off()

Figure 1: PCA plot of the variance in our samples. Nontumor samples (red) cluster nicely to the rightmost side of the plot. Less clustering is observed among the tumor samples (blue), which is to be expected as tumor involve many mutations, randomly “generated” from a millions of potential genome errors. Our tumor samples cluster to the leftmost side of the plot. Differences among the tumor samples only account for 14% of the total variance among all samples. We will continue to see the lack of strong clustering among the tumor samples reflected in later figures.

5.2 Generate a correlation heatmap

#pdf(file = "correlation_plot_TNT_RNAseq.pdf", height = 3, width = 4)

vst_mat <- assay(vstcounts)
vst_cor <- cor(vst_mat)

#annotation_heatmap <- data.frame(row.names=colnames(vst_mat), condition)

cor_heatmap <- as.ggplot(pheatmap(vst_cor,
                                  main = "sample correlation", 
                                  show_rownames = F, 
                                  show_colnames = T,
                                  fontsize = 6,
                                  fontsize_col = 6,
                                  fontsize_row = 6,
                                  width = 3,
                                  height = 4))

#dev.off()

Figure 2: Correlation heatmap of our samples. Correlation values range from about 0.8 (dark blue) to 1 (red). Correlation across samples is higher among nontumor samples than among tumor samples and between tumor and nontumor samples. This is expected as nontumor or healthy samples will have greater similarity among themselves, being normal healthy liver cells, than the tumor samples which have many random mutations each.

6 Post-Processing, Part 1

6.1 Compare and Contrast

# How does gene regulation differ between the tumor and nontumor sample groups?
tum_non_res <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "tumor", "nontumor"))

head(tum_non_res)
## log2 fold change (MLE): condition tumor vs nontumor 
## Wald test p-value: condition tumor vs nontumor 
## DataFrame with 6 rows and 6 columns
##                  baseMean log2FoldChange     lfcSE      stat      pvalue
##                 <numeric>      <numeric> <numeric> <numeric>   <numeric>
## ENSG00000000003  308.1936     -0.6819711  0.422167 -1.615406 0.106222835
## ENSG00000000419   80.5779      0.2445667  0.384466  0.636121 0.524697655
## ENSG00000000457   44.9737      0.4179315  0.496099  0.842436 0.399543771
## ENSG00000000460   11.9269      1.7969929  0.720096  2.495492 0.012578261
## ENSG00000000938   25.3287     -2.1209641  0.554476 -3.825172 0.000130681
## ENSG00000000971 1059.5147      0.0813659  0.579654  0.140370 0.888367936
##                       padj
##                  <numeric>
## ENSG00000000003 0.36659696
## ENSG00000000419 0.78699770
## ENSG00000000457 0.70652193
## ENSG00000000460 0.10659155
## ENSG00000000938 0.00425914
## ENSG00000000971 0.96306692

6.2 Merge results

tum_non_res <- merge(as.data.frame(tum_non_res), 
                 as.data.frame(counts(dds, normalized=TRUE)), 
                 by="row.names", 
                 sort=FALSE)

names(tum_non_res)[1] <- "Gene_id"

str(tum_non_res)
## 'data.frame':    23258 obs. of  16 variables:
##  $ Gene_id           : 'AsIs' chr  "ENSG00000000003" "ENSG00000000419" "ENSG00000000457" "ENSG00000000460" ...
##  $ baseMean          : num  308.2 80.6 45 11.9 25.3 ...
##  $ log2FoldChange    : num  -0.682 0.245 0.418 1.797 -2.121 ...
##  $ lfcSE             : num  0.422 0.384 0.496 0.72 0.554 ...

##  $ Tumor T_b_016     : num  122.62 94.32 27.25 14.67 5.24 ...
##  $ Tumor T_c_022     : num  128.68 117.49 39.16 33.57 5.59 ...
##  $ Tumor T_d_024     : num  291.8 79.7 58 12.9 11.2 ...
##  $ Tumor T_f_029     : num  373.1 70.4 83.2 21.8 10.5 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_aa_015: num  354.27 44.56 24.51 2.23 57.93 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_bb_016: num  250.7 59.4 46.2 0 46.2 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_cc_022: num  394.7 105 50.4 4.2 31.1 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_dd_024: num  437.67 75.69 26.33 9.87 16.45 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_ff_029: num  420.21 78.63 49.68 8.05 43.8 ...

6.3 Sort changes in gene expression by up-regulation, down-regulation, or unchanged

#Add a column to dataframe to specify up or down regulated
tum_non_res$diffexpressed <- "Unchanged"

#if log2FC > 1 and padj < 0.05 set as upregulated
tum_non_res$diffexpressed[tum_non_res$padj < 0.05 & tum_non_res$log2FoldChange > 1] <- "Upregulated"

#if log2FC > -1 and padj < 0.05 set as downregulated
tum_non_res$diffexpressed[tum_non_res$padj < 0.05 & tum_non_res$log2FoldChange < -1] <- "Downregulated"

str(tum_non_res)
## 'data.frame':    23258 obs. of  17 variables:
##  $ Gene_id           : 'AsIs' chr  "ENSG00000000003" "ENSG00000000419" "ENSG00000000457" "ENSG00000000460" ...
##  $ baseMean          : num  308.2 80.6 45 11.9 25.3 ...
##  $ log2FoldChange    : num  -0.682 0.245 0.418 1.797 -2.121 ...
##  $ lfcSE             : num  0.422 0.384 0.496 0.72 0.554 ...
##  $ stat              : num  -1.615 0.636 0.842 2.495 -3.825 ...
##  $ pvalue            : num  0.106223 0.524698 0.399544 0.012578 0.000131 ...
##  $ padj              : num  0.3666 0.787 0.70652 0.10659 0.00426 ...
##  $ Tumor T_b_016     : num  122.62 94.32 27.25 14.67 5.24 ...
##  $ Tumor T_c_022     : num  128.68 117.49 39.16 33.57 5.59 ...
##  $ Tumor T_d_024     : num  291.8 79.7 58 12.9 11.2 ...
##  $ Tumor T_f_029     : num  373.1 70.4 83.2 21.8 10.5 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_aa_015: num  354.27 44.56 24.51 2.23 57.93 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_bb_016: num  250.7 59.4 46.2 0 46.2 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_cc_022: num  394.7 105 50.4 4.2 31.1 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_dd_024: num  437.67 75.69 26.33 9.87 16.45 ...
##  $ Non Tumor T_ff_029: num  420.21 78.63 49.68 8.05 43.8 ...
##  $ diffexpressed     : chr  "Unchanged" "Unchanged" "Unchanged" "Unchanged" ...
tum_non_full_matrix <- read.csv(file = "Tumor_Vs_Nontumor_full_matrix_042225.csv",
                                header = TRUE)

tum_non_full_matrix <- tibble(tum_non_full_matrix)
# changed data_frame() to tibble()

7 Data Visualization, Part 2

#Add a label column to the result table 
genes_to_label <- c("CD4", "MAPK10", "GPBAR1", "ABCC4") # genes we chose later in our analysis 

tum_non_full_matrix$label <- ifelse(tum_non_full_matrix$symbol %in% genes_to_label,
                                   tum_non_full_matrix$symbol,
                                   NA)
ggplot(tum_non_full_matrix) +
  geom_point(aes(x = log2FoldChange, 
                 y = -log10(padj), 
                 color = diffexpressed, 
                 alpha = 0.8)) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(x = log2FoldChange, 
                      y = -log10(padj), 
                      label = label),
                  size = 3, 
                  max.overlaps = Inf) +
  theme_classic() + 
  ggtitle("Tumor vs NonTumor") +
  xlab("Log2 fold change") + 
  ylab("-Log10(padj)") +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title = element_text(size = rel(1.25), hjust = 0.5), 
        axis.title = element_text(size = rel(1.25))) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("lightblue", "grey", "pink"),
                     labels = c("Downregulated", "Not Significant", "Upregulated")) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-15, 15)) + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 20), clip = "off")

Figure 3: Volcano plot of the change in gene regulation in our tumor samples vs our nontumor samples. Genes selected later for closer analysis are highlighted (CD4, MAPK10, GPBAR1, and ABCC4). There are many genes both up-regulated and down-regulated within the samples. Cancerous cells are quite different from noncancerous cells and as such both cell types will have large difference in their level and type of gene regulation. This is not a surprising result.

8 Data Visualization, Part 3

We want to replicate Figure 4b and 4C from our paper. Figure 4B is a GO enrichment analysis, and Figure 4C is a Pathway analysis.

8.1 Prepping the Data

# load in previously saved full .csv file
TNT_data <- read.csv("Tumor_Vs_Nontumor_full_matrix_042225.csv", header = T)

head(TNT_data)
##           Gene_id   baseMean log2FoldChange     lfcSE       stat       pvalue
## 1 ENSG00000000003  308.19359    -0.68197107 0.4221670 -1.6154058 0.1062228345
## 2 ENSG00000000419   80.57793     0.24456668 0.3844657  0.6361209 0.5246976553
## 3 ENSG00000000457   44.97367     0.41793148 0.4960986  0.8424363 0.3995437706
## 4 ENSG00000000460   11.92686     1.79699286 0.7200956  2.4954919 0.0125782615
## 5 ENSG00000000938   25.32872    -2.12096410 0.5544755 -3.8251718 0.0001306809
## 6 ENSG00000000971 1059.51471     0.08136588 0.5796543  0.1403696 0.8883679365
##          padj Tumor.T_b_016 Tumor.T_c_022 Tumor.T_d_024 Tumor.T_f_029
## 1 0.366596959    122.618636    128.683423     291.84512     373.06914
## 2 0.786997701     94.322028    117.493560      79.73406      70.43232
## 3 0.706521928     27.248586     39.164520      58.03038      83.23819
## 4 0.106591553     14.672315     33.569589      12.92985      21.82226
## 5 0.004259141      5.240113      5.594931      11.23665      10.45378
## 6 0.963066915    444.361553   1309.213958     594.46510    2018.10152
##   Non.Tumor.T_aa_015 Non.Tumor.T_bb_016 Non.Tumor.T_cc_022 Non.Tumor.T_dd_024
## 1         354.265954          250.66055         394.718134         437.669642
## 2          44.561755           59.36697         104.978227          75.687231
## 3          24.508965           46.17431          50.389549          26.325993
## 4           2.228088            0.00000           4.199129           9.872248
## 5          57.930282           46.17431          31.073555          16.453746
## 6        1713.399491         1715.04586         513.973400         819.396548
##   Non.Tumor.T_ff_029 diffexpressed symbol
## 1         420.211682     Unchanged TSPAN6
## 2          78.625243     Unchanged   DPM1
## 3          49.682486     Unchanged  SCYL3
## 4           8.048253     Unchanged  FIRRM
## 5          43.801070 Downregulated    FGR
## 6         407.674980     Unchanged    CFH
# pull out columns we need for figure construction
TNT_background <- dplyr::select(TNT_data, 
                                Gene_id,
                                log2FoldChange,
                                padj,
                                symbol)
# create a list of significant genes based on log2 fold change
original_gene_list <- TNT_background$log2FoldChange
head(original_gene_list)
## [1] -0.68197107  0.24456668  0.41793148  1.79699286 -2.12096410  0.08136588
# name the vector
names(original_gene_list) <- TNT_background$Ensembl_id
head(original_gene_list)
## [1] -0.68197107  0.24456668  0.41793148  1.79699286 -2.12096410  0.08136588
#original_gene_list
# remove any rows with N/A values
background_gene_list<-na.omit(original_gene_list)
# sort the list in decreasing order (this is required for clusterProfiler)
background_gene_list = sort(background_gene_list, decreasing = TRUE)
head(background_gene_list)
## [1] 24.71824 24.17016 23.46051 23.04766 22.89614 22.75596

Now we want to pull out all genes that have significant results

# Extract significant results (padj < 0.05) using subset()
#sig_genes_df = subset(TNT_background, padj < 0.05)

# From significant results, we want to filter on log2fold change
#sig_genes <- sig_genes_df$log2FoldChange

#sig_genes <- tibble(sig_genes)

# Name the vector 
#names(sig_genes) <- sig_genes_df$Ensembl_id

# omit NA values
#ig_genes <- na.omit(sig_genes)

# filter on min log2fold change (log2FoldChange > 1)
#sig_genes <- names(sig_genes)[abs(sig_genes) > 1]

The above chunk of code was being finicky, so we used the chunk below to pull out our significant genes…

# Step 1: Filter significant genes
sig_genes_df <- subset(TNT_background, padj < 0.05)

# Step 2: Remove NAs from log2FoldChange
sig_genes_df <- sig_genes_df[!is.na(sig_genes_df$log2FoldChange), ]

# Step 3: Filter on abs(log2FoldChange) > 1
sig_genes_df <- sig_genes_df[abs(sig_genes_df$log2FoldChange) > 1, ]

# Step 4: Extract Ensembl IDs of filtered genes
sig_genes <- sig_genes_df$Gene_id
head(sig_genes)
## [1] "ENSG00000000938" "ENSG00000001626" "ENSG00000005001" "ENSG00000005187"
## [5] "ENSG00000005421" "ENSG00000005471"

8.2 Gene Ontology (GO) Enrichment Analysis

Now we can generate Figure 4C

# specify source of gene id annotations
#go_enrich <- enrichGO(gene = sig_genes,
                      #universe = 'sig_genes_df',
                      #OrgDb = 'org.Hs.eg.db', 
                      #keyType = 'ENSEMBL',
                      #readable = T,
                      #ont = "BP",
                      #pvalueCutoff = 0.05, 
                      #qvalueCutoff = 0.05)

Unfortunately, none of our genes sets have a size between 10 and 500, this returns a “NULL” result. We cannot generate Figure 4B

8.3 KEGG pathway

Now we want to prepare our dataset for creating Figure 4B, our pathway analysis

# Create vector of log2FoldChange values
kegg_gene_list <- TNT_background$log2FoldChange

# Assign ENTREZ IDs as names
names(kegg_gene_list) <- TNT_background$Gene_id

# Remove NA values
kegg_gene_list <- na.omit(kegg_gene_list)

# Remove duplicates in ENTREZ IDs (keep the first occurrence)
kegg_gene_list <- kegg_gene_list[!duplicated(names(kegg_gene_list))]

# sort the list in decreasing order (required for clusterProfiler)
kegg_gene_list = sort(kegg_gene_list, decreasing = TRUE)

head(kegg_gene_list)
## ENSG00000170373 ENSG00000238269 ENSG00000283599 ENSG00000158639 ENSG00000126752 
##        24.71824        24.17016        23.46051        23.04766        22.89614 
## ENSG00000234722 
##        22.75596

The above code chunk just created a background list of genes, similar to what we did above. Now, we will create the significant gene list.

# Step 1: Filter significant genes
kegg_sig_genes_df <- subset(TNT_background, padj < 0.05)

# Step 2: Remove NAs from log2FoldChange
kegg_sig_genes_df <- kegg_sig_genes_df[!is.na(kegg_sig_genes_df$log2FoldChange), ]

# Step 3: Filter on abs(log2FoldChange) > 1
kegg_sig_genes_df <- kegg_sig_genes_df[abs(kegg_sig_genes_df$log2FoldChange) > 1, ]

# Step 4: Extract Ensembl IDs of filtered genes
kegg_sig_genes <- kegg_sig_genes_df$Gene_id

head(kegg_sig_genes)
## [1] "ENSG00000000938" "ENSG00000001626" "ENSG00000005001" "ENSG00000005187"
## [5] "ENSG00000005421" "ENSG00000005471"

Use clusterProfiler to search for supported organisms with search_kegg_organism()

#sal_ent <- search_kegg_organism('Homo sapiens',
                                #by='scientific_name')

#dim(sal_ent)

Now we will visualize the KEGG pathways for our significant genes

#kegg_organism = "hsa"
#kk_TNT <- enrichKEGG(gene=sig_genes_TNT,
                     #universe = names(background_gene_list),
                     #organism = kegg_organism,
                     #pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
                     #keyType = "ncbi-geneid")

Unfortunately, we were unable to visualize the KEGG pathways for our significant genes. We were unable to generate Figure 4C.

8.4 Visulize HCC pathway

To double check that our significant genes are even related to Human Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC), we will visualize the pathway hsa05225, which is the HCC pathway for Homo sapiens

hsa05225 <- pathview(gene.data  = kegg_gene_list,
                     pathway.id = "hsa05225",
                     species    = "hsa",
                     limit      = list(gene=max(abs(kegg_gene_list)), cpd=1))
## Warning: None of the genes or compounds mapped to the pathway!
## Argument gene.idtype or cpd.idtype may be wrong.
## 'select()' returned 1:1 mapping between keys and columns
## Info: Working in directory /gpfs1/home/j/m/jmajeske/mmg3320_final_proj/counts
## Info: Writing image file hsa05225.pathview.png
knitr::include_graphics("hsa05225.pathview.png")

Figure 4: KEGG pathway for Human Hepatocelluar Carcinoma (HCC). Our significant genes did not align neatly with the HCC pathway. This would explain why our previous attempts to make a GO enrichment analysis and a pathway analysis returned NULL results. Something is wrong with the data or the processing and visualization.

9 Visulaization, Part 4

9.1 Make Boxplots…

According to the study, increased expression of CD6 and MAPK10 were associated with healthy samples while GPBAR1 is associated with HHC tumors and ABCC4 is highly expressed in the presenc of bile acid, regardless of tumor or nontumor status. We expect to see an increase in the average number of reads for CD6 and MAPK10 in our nontumor samples compared to our tumor samples. We also expect to see an increase in the number of reads for GPBAR1 in our tumor samples compared to our nontumor samples. We expect that ABCC4 read count will remain roughly even across the board.

We are going to make a simple scatter plot and then a dotplot for each of our chosen genes. For future reference, sizeFactor = how many reads there are in a library

9.2 CD4

CD4 is supposed to be up-regulated in nontumor samples…

# make the scatter plot
plotCounts(dds, "ENSG00000013725")

# make the boxplot

#pdf(file = "CD6_boxplot.pdf", height = 2.5, width = 6)
CD6 <- plotCounts(dds,
                  gene = "ENSG00000013725",
                  intgroup = c("condition", "sizeFactor"),  
                  returnData = TRUE) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, 
             y = sizeFactor, 
             fill = sizeFactor)) + 
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, color = "black") + 
  geom_jitter(shape = 16, position = position_jitter(0.2)) +
  labs(title = "CD6 expression", 
       y = "Size Factor", 
       x = "Condition") + 
  theme_grey() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14)) +
  facet_grid(~condition)
#dev.off()

print(CD6)

9.3 MAPK10

MAPK10 is supposed to be up-regulated in nontumor samples…

# make the scatter plot
plotCounts(dds, "ENSG00000109339")

# make the boxplot

#pdf(file = "MAPK10_boxplot.pdf", height = 2.5, width = 6)
MAPK10 <- plotCounts(dds,
                  gene = "ENSG00000109339",
                  intgroup = c("sizeFactor", "condition"),  
                  returnData = TRUE) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, 
             y = sizeFactor, 
             fill = sizeFactor)) + 
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, color = "black") + 
  geom_jitter(shape = 16, position = position_jitter(0.2)) +
  labs(title = "MAPK10 expression", 
       y = "Size Factor", 
       x = "Condition") + 
  theme_grey() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14)) +
  facet_grid(~condition)
#dev.off()

print(MAPK10)

9.4 GPBAR1

GPBAR1 has functions in tumorigenesis of hepatocytes and mesenchymal immune cells. It is supposed to be up-regulated in tumor samples…

# make the scatter plot
plotCounts(dds, "ENSG00000179921")

# make the boxplot

#pdf(file = "GPBAR1_boxplot.pdf", height = 2.5, width = 6)
GPBAR1 <- plotCounts(dds,
                  gene = "ENSG00000179921",
                  intgroup = c("sizeFactor", "condition"),  
                  returnData = TRUE) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, 
             y = sizeFactor, 
             fill = sizeFactor)) + 
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, color = "black") + 
  geom_jitter(shape = 16, position = position_jitter(0.2)) +
  labs(title = "GPBAR1 expression", 
       y = "Size Factor", 
       x = "Condition") + 
  theme_grey() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14)) +
  facet_grid(~condition)
#dev.off()

print(GPBAR1)

9.5 ABCC4

ABCC4 is expressed in both tumor and nontumor in patients with high levels of bile acid, which may make bile acid a mediator of the communication between gut microbiota and HCC transcriptome, we expect ABCC4 to be equaly expressed in both the tumor and nontumor samples…

# make the scatter plot
plotCounts(dds, "ENSG00000125257")

# make the boxplot

#pdf(file = "ABCC4_boxplot.pdf", height = 2.5, width = 6)
ABCC4 <- plotCounts(dds,
                  gene = "ENSG00000125257",
                  intgroup = c("sizeFactor", "condition"),  
                  returnData = TRUE) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, 
             y = sizeFactor, 
             fill = sizeFactor)) + 
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, color = "black") + 
  geom_jitter(shape = 16, position = position_jitter(0.2)) +
  labs(title = "ABCC4 expression", 
       y = "Size Factor", 
       x = "Condition") + 
  theme_grey() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14)) +
  facet_grid(~condition)
#dev.off()

print(ABCC4)

10 Assemble a Multi-panel Figure

We are going to combine all our barplots into one figure, both to make them easier to compare to each other and to group this data.

boxplot_grid <- plot_grid(CD6,
                          MAPK10,
                          GPBAR1,
                          ABCC4,
                          ncol = 3,
                          rel_widths = c(1, 1, 1),
                          labels = NA)
print(boxplot_grid)

Figure 5: Boxplots of selected genes. SizeFactor indicates the numb er of reads in each sample that correspond to the selected gene of interest for each boxplot. All tumor boxplots were larger than their corresponding nontumor boxplots (which were also all about the same size). This is explained by the larger variance among tumor samples compared to the nontumor samples that was observed in Figure 1. Visually, all 4 boxplots look nearly identical with only very minor details differing between them. This may indicate that something is wrong with our data or with out processing steps. We expected to see on average more reads that corresponded to CD6 and MAPK10 in our nontumor samples compared to the tumor samples. This was the opposite of what was observed. A similar pattern was seen with our GPBAR1 boxplot, which was what we expected to see. However, taken in context with our other very similar boxplots we cannot assume that this figure is valid. Lastly, we expected that the read counts for ABCC4, which is highly expressed in bile salts would remain the same between our tumor and nontumor samples as both set of samples were taken from the liver and were (presumably) exposed to teh same or similar levels of bile salts. This was not what was observed. Again, the high degree of similarity across all 4 of our selected genes, two of which are known to be associated with healthy tissues (CD4 and MAPK10), one of which is known to be associated with HHC tumors (GPBAR1), and one of which responds to an environmental condition (ABCC4) indicates that something is not right with this data and/or this processing and visualization.

11 Conclusions

We experienced many roadblocks during our analysis. Most significantly, were were unable to generate any of the figures we set out to recreate. We suspect that somewhere along our processing pipeline something has gone wrong. Alternatively, this could be due to our original data set. MultiQC of our samples was nearly flawless, an excellent start, but during our R-based post-processing things would not stop breaking. Upon digging further into the paper details we discovered a couple inconsistencies and other “red flags” that made us question the integrity of the study. Firstly, the title of the paper “Retrospective gene expression analysis of human RNA samples from Hepatocellular Carcinoma in relation with survival” and the abstract implies that the researchers determined specific RNA samples and corresponding gut microbes that correspond to the development of Human Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC). They did not do this. Secondly, they collected two types of samples; liver tumor and nontumor samples, which we were looking at, and fecal samples. The liver and fecal samples each underwent different experiments and bioinformatic processing and experimentation. In fact, the two sets of samples were so divorced from each other that the results and discussion sections of the paper read like two unrelated papers spliced together at random. This made for a very confusing paper to read. Lastly, almost as if to cover for their total lack of concrete results, the paper was written with far too much scientific jargon, an overuse of which further confused their results and prevented understanding. The authors do discuss that a major limitation in their study was the retrospective nature of it. Samples could only be collected from a single time point, so changes in a host’s gut microbiome as they develop and recover from HCC could not be compared. They expressed no concrete next steps, but we can only assume that they would like to repeat this investigation with tumor and nontumor samples from patients as they make their way through a recovery from HCC. We agree with the authors, and think that a study of narrower scope with current patients with HCC could reveal much about the way the gut microbiome changes throughout a HCC patient’s recovery. Focusing on just the first component of their investigation (how does the gut microbiome change during patients experiencing HCC compared to noncancerous patients?) and expanding the scope of the study in another way (over multiple time points) would provide much more meaningful data. Unfortunately, we do not trust any of the results we did mange to get with our bioinformatic analysis. Our readcounts boxplots were nearly identical across the board even when we anticipated different patterns. The high degree of similarity signals that there may be something further upstream of the result is incorrect. We do not know where precisely things got fouled up, and so would like to re-attempt to generate figure 4B and 4C from the paper using all the relevant samples from their published data set and following exactly their processing pipeline. If their figures cannot be replicated, the validity of the study should be questioned.
